note: Single run per cell (n=1, own subprocess each), peak working set via psutil peak_wset on Windows. Memory is the claim; timings are secondary. Absolute MB will differ on your machine — the cross-engine ratios and the reducing-vs-row-preserving pattern are the durable findings.

metrics.label: Experiment A — group-by aggregate, 30M rows -> 2,000 groups (scan_parquet.group_by.agg(len,sum,mean,max).sort)
metrics.ratio_desc: streaming uses 2.8x less peak memory than polars in-memory and 6.7x less than pandas on the same reducing query; checksums identical across all three.

metrics.label: Experiment C — same engine="streaming" flag, different ops (the headline honest limit)
metrics.pattern: Row-REDUCING queries (30M -> 2k): streaming bounds memory, 2.8-3x less. Row-PRESERVING queries (30M -> 30M): no streaming advantage — window_over is slightly WORSE than in-memory (1050 vs 931MB) and full_sort is a wash (~2.7GB both). Peak memory is governed by rows-out, not by the engine flag.

findings.claim: engine="streaming" genuinely bounds memory for reducing queries on larger-than-RAM-style scans.
findings.detail: group-by over 30M rows peaks at 366MB streaming vs 1035MB in-memory vs 2466MB pandas, identical checksum. Join+agg: 667 vs 2031MB.

findings.claim: engine="streaming" is NOT a magic memory cap — row-preserving ops gain nothing.
findings.detail: Same flag, window_over peaks at 1050MB (in-memory: 931MB) and full_sort at 2723MB (in-memory: 2691MB). Official docs state some ops are inherently non-streaming or not yet implemented streaming and FALL BACK to the in-memory engine — consistent with these measurements.

findings.claim: streaming and in-memory results are numerically equal but not bit-identical.
findings.detail: DataFrame.equals() == False. Per column: key/n/val_max bit-exact; val_sum max_abs_diff 1.024e-08 (1392/2000 groups differ); val_mean max_abs_diff 6.821e-13. All within rel-tol 1e-9. Cause: float addition is non-associative and streaming combines chunk partial sums in a different order. Regression tests comparing engines need approximate equality (check_exact=False).

findings.claim: The new streaming engine is opt-in, not the default, as of 1.42.1.
findings.detail: collect(engine="streaming") or pl.Config.set_engine_affinity("streaming"). Official docs: 'not the default' / 'will become the default in time'. Development tracked in pola-rs/polars#20947 since ~1.31.

explicitly_not_verified.note: probe.py's OPS includes rank_over; its measurement was not retained in the session record, so it is not reported here. Re-run `probe.py measop rank_over streaming` to measure it.

Which specific ops internally fell back to the in-memory engine was NOT instrumented (the fallback is silent). The fallback explanation comes from official docs; the measurements are peak-memory observations only.
The 87MB parquet fits in RAM. This run demonstrates memory BOUNDING behavior per engine, not a genuinely larger-than-RAM out-of-core workload.
Windows only. peak_wset is Windows-specific; Linux/macOS would need a peak-RSS equivalent (e.g. ru_maxrss).
